claim: Skills are folders containing not just markdown files but also scripts, assets, and data that agents can discover and manipulate, contrary to the common misconception they are only markdown
actionable: When building agent skills, organize them as full folders with supporting scripts and data assets, not just documentation files
source_channel: Brock Mesarich | AI for Non Techies
source_title: Anthropic Just Dropped Their Claude Skills Secrets (steal these)

claim: The 'gotchas' section is Anthropic's highest-signal content in skills - it should capture common failure points and what NOT to do, updated as the model encounters edge cases
actionable: Build a comprehensive 'gotchas' section in agent skills documenting common failure modes and anti-patterns, treating it as the most critical skill component
source_channel: Brock Mesarich | AI for Non Techies
source_title: Anthropic Just Dropped Their Claude Skills Secrets (steal these)

claim: Write skill descriptions for the model, not for humans - descriptions trigger skill execution, so they should explain when to use the skill, not summarize what it does
actionable: Rewrite agent skill descriptions as 'when to trigger' statements that activate model reasoning, not human-readable summaries
source_channel: Brock Mesarich | AI for Non Techies
source_title: Anthropic Just Dropped Their Claude Skills Secrets (steal these)

claim: Avoid railroading Claude with overly prescriptive step-by-step instructions; instead give it the task goal and information needed, allowing it to find the best approach
actionable: Replace detailed procedural instructions with outcome-focused prompts that let the model adapt its approach to the specific situation
source_channel: Brock Mesarich | AI for Non Techies
source_title: Anthropic Just Dropped Their Claude Skills Secrets (steal these)

claim: Anthropic's best skills started as a few lines with one gotcha, then evolved as Claude hit new edge cases - start small and iterate rather than building perfect skills upfront
actionable: Start with minimal skill definitions (a few lines plus one gotcha section), deploy them, and incrementally improve as you encounter real-world edge cases
